question_text: Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) differs fundamentally from portfolio investment (buying stocks and bonds) as a mode of international capital flow. What is the most politically significant distinction between FDI and portfolio investment from the perspective of host country governments and development economists?

options.A: FDI always benefits the host country while portfolio investment always harms it - there is no case where FDI has produced negative outcomes for recipients
options.B: FDI involves long-term commitment of capital with management control, technology transfer, and supply chain integration that creates real productive assets - while portfolio investment is footloose capital that can exit rapidly during crises, creating systemic instability without corresponding productive investment
options.C: Portfolio investment is preferred by developing countries because it brings more capital than FDI and allows host governments to retain full management control over enterprises
options.D: FDI and portfolio investment are legally identical - the only difference is that FDI requires approval by the host country's central bank while portfolio flows are automatic

explanation: The FDI-portfolio distinction is fundamental in international finance and development economics. FDI (conventionally defined as acquiring 10% or more of voting shares in a foreign enterprise) involves: physical capital formation (factories, equipment), technology and knowledge transfer (production methods, management practices), integration into global supply chains, employment creation, and typically multi-year commitment horizons. Even when FDI is problematic (transfer pricing to minimise taxes, environmental concerns, labour exploitation), it creates real productive assets. Portfolio investment (buying shares below the 10% threshold, bonds, or derivatives) can be executed and liquidated in milliseconds through computerised trading. The 1997-98 Asian Financial Crisis demonstrated the 'sudden stop' problem: when US interest rates rose and risk appetite fell, $105 billion of portfolio capital that had flowed into Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea, and Malaysia in 1996-97 reversed rapidly, collapsing exchange rates and triggering banking crises. Countries like Malaysia that imposed capital controls on portfolio outflows (Mahathir's decision in September 1998) recovered more quickly than those following IMF prescriptions. Jagdish Bhagwati distinguished between 'capital account liberalisation' (opening portfolio flows) which he argued was dangerous and 'trade liberalisation' which he defended - a notable dissent from standard Washington Consensus positions.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: FDI always benefits host countries (A) is empirically refuted by transfer pricing abuses (Apple, Google minimising tax through Ireland and Netherlands), environmental damage from extractive FDI (oil in Nigeria), and export processing zones with suppressed labour rights; FDI has real costs that complicate simple benefit narratives

Portfolio preferred by developing countries (C) reverses actual policy - most developing countries actively seek FDI (stable, productive) while managing portfolio flows carefully; sudden reversals of portfolio capital caused the 1997 Asian crisis, 1994 Mexico crisis, and 2013 'taper tantrum' - all crisis-triggering sudden stops

Legal identity of FDI and portfolio (D) is wrong - the 10% ownership threshold, IMF Balance of Payments Manual definitions, OECD Benchmark Definition, and legal treatment in bilateral investment treaties all distinguish the two; they have separate regulatory frameworks in most countries including India's FEMA

question_text: The 2008 Global Financial Crisis originated in US subprime mortgage markets but rapidly spread globally, becoming the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression. Which sequence of mechanisms most accurately explains how a US housing market problem became a global economic catastrophe?

options.A: US mortgage defaults triggered a trade war with China which reduced global exports, causing a simultaneous GDP contraction in all G20 countries
options.B: Securitisation of subprime mortgages into complex instruments (CDOs, MBS) sold globally distributed toxic assets across the world's financial system; interconnected bank balance sheets meant losses in US housing became losses for European banks; Lehman Brothers' bankruptcy (September 2008) triggered a global credit freeze; capital flows reversed to emerging markets causing simultaneous recession
options.C: The US Federal Reserve's interest rate increases in 2007 caused a global currency crisis as all developing country currencies lost value simultaneously against the dollar
options.D: The 2008 crisis was confined to the United States and United Kingdom - other countries experienced slowdowns only because they chose to adopt similar bank bailout policies rather than because of genuine financial contagion

explanation: The 2008 crisis transmission mechanism illustrates financial globalisation's systemic risk. The sequence: (1) Securitisation: US mortgage lenders originated subprime loans with minimal underwriting standards (NINJA loans - no income, no job, no assets), packaged them into Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS) and Collateralised Debt Obligations (CDOs), which received AAA ratings from agencies that used flawed models. These instruments were sold globally - European banks (UBS, Deutsche Bank, Royal Bank of Scotland), Asian sovereign wealth funds, and US money market funds all held them. (2) Asset price collapse: When US house prices fell from 2006, defaults rose sharply. The assumption in CDO models that US regional housing markets were uncorrelated proved wrong as a national price decline occurred simultaneously. (3) Interbank contagion: Banks stopped trusting each other's balance sheets because no one knew who held how much toxic debt. The TED spread (measuring bank credit risk) spiked. Northern Rock (UK) experienced the first bank run since the 19th century. (4) Lehman collapse: Lehman Brothers' Chapter 11 filing on September 15, 2008 froze global credit markets - $600 billion in liabilities across 80+ countries suddenly defaulted. Money market funds 'broke the buck'. (5) Emerging market contagion: Portfolio capital reversed from emerging markets as investors deleveraged globally; Iceland's entire banking system collapsed; Eastern European currencies fell; even India saw Sensex fall 60% and rupee depreciate. The global recession was the deepest since the 1930s.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Trade war with China as mechanism (A) reverses causality - the 2008 crisis caused trade to collapse (global trade fell 12% in 2009) but there was no trade war trigger; US-China trade tensions escalated under Trump (2018) a decade later; financial contagion through interconnected banks, not trade wars, was the 2008 transmission mechanism

Confined to US and UK (D) is empirically false - Iceland's GDP fell 10%, Latvia's fell 18%, Ireland required a bailout; South Korea's won depreciated 40%; India's Sensex fell 60% from peak; the claim that others chose similar policies ignores documented capital flow reversals and trade collapse that affected all major economies

Fed rate increases causing global currency crisis (C) conflates 2007 Fed actions with an unrelated mechanism - Fed funds rate actually fell rapidly from 2007 as the crisis emerged; the 2013 'taper tantrum' (when Bernanke suggested tapering QE) did cause emerging market currency stress, but this postdates 2008 and operates differently

question_text: Multinational corporations (MNCs) account for approximately one-third of global GDP and two-thirds of world trade. The practice of 'transfer pricing' is one of the most controversial aspects of MNC operations in developing countries. What is transfer pricing and why does it matter for development finance?

options.A: Transfer pricing refers to MNCs transferring their headquarters from one country to another to exploit lower tax rates - the practice of corporate inversion
options.B: Transfer pricing involves MNCs setting prices for transactions between their own subsidiaries in different countries - when done to shift profits from high-tax to low-tax jurisdictions, it reduces tax revenues in developing countries hosting productive operations, creating a form of capital flight through accounting manipulation
options.C: Transfer pricing is an international accounting standard developed by the IASB that requires MNCs to price all cross-border transactions at market rates, ensuring fair tax distribution between countries
options.D: Transfer pricing refers to the practice of local companies transferring their assets to MNC ownership in exchange for technology - it is a legally mandated requirement under FDI treaties

explanation: Transfer pricing is the price set for transactions between related entities (subsidiaries, parent companies) within the same MNC group. When a pharmaceutical company based in the US manufactures a drug in India using a patent owned by an Irish subsidiary, the Indian operation pays royalties to Ireland (low tax) reducing Indian taxable profits; simultaneously the Indian operation sells the drug to a Brazilian subsidiary at below-market prices, shifting profit to Brazil's zero-tax free trade zone. The arm's length principle (requiring related-party prices to match what unrelated parties would charge) is the international standard (OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines, UN Practical Manual on Transfer Pricing) - but enforcement is technically complex because many transactions between subsidiaries involve unique assets (brand value, patents) with no comparable market prices. The OECD BEPS (Base Erosion and Profit Shifting) project, launched 2013 and producing the BEPS Action Plan (15 action items), was designed to address these abuses. Estimates by Global Financial Integrity and Tax Justice Network suggest developing countries lose $100-200 billion annually through illicit financial flows including transfer pricing manipulation - exceeding total ODA flows to many regions. India has a Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO) system within the Income Tax Department and has conducted many high-profile transfer pricing adjustments against companies including IBM, Microsoft, Shell, and Vodafone.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Corporate inversion (A) is a related but distinct tax avoidance strategy where a company reincorporates in a low-tax jurisdiction while maintaining US operations - different from transfer pricing; inversion changes the headquarters; transfer pricing manipulates internal transaction prices without moving headquarters

IASB accounting standard (C) reverses reality - transfer pricing is a tax problem OECD/UN are trying to solve; it is not an IASB standard requiring arm's length pricing; the arm's length standard exists as a principle but its enforcement is the difficulty; describing transfer pricing as already solved by a proper standard misrepresents the ongoing policy challenge

Mandatory asset transfer to MNCs (D) describes a fictional requirement with no basis in reality - no FDI treaty requires local companies to transfer assets to MNC ownership; if anything, host countries restrict MNC control of strategic assets; this option conflates investment liberalisation with an invented legal obligation

question_text: Remittances - money sent by migrant workers back to their home countries - have become one of the most significant financial flows in the global economy. According to World Bank data, which country has been the world's largest remittance recipient in recent years, and why do remittances matter for development?

options.A: China is the world's largest remittance recipient; remittances fund China's Belt and Road Initiative through diaspora investment channels
options.B: India is the world's largest remittance recipient (approximately $111 billion in 2022); remittances provide counter-cyclical income support to households, fund education and healthcare consumption, and exceed official development assistance flows to many developing countries
options.C: The United States is the world's largest remittance recipient because it attracts the most skilled migrants who send earnings home after working in American technology companies
options.D: Mexico is the world's largest remittance recipient and receives more remittances than any other country because of the size of the US-Mexico migration corridor

explanation: India has been the world's largest remittance recipient since 2008, receiving approximately $111 billion in 2022 (World Bank Migration and Development Brief, 2022) - with Mexico second at $61 billion and China third at approximately $51 billion. India's dominant position reflects: (1) Scale of diaspora: approximately 32 million Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) globally, with major concentrations in the Gulf (UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Qatar, Oman, Bahrain), USA, UK, Canada, and Australia; (2) Gulf migration: millions of blue-collar workers in the Gulf send a large proportion of their earnings home; Kerala, UP, Bihar, and Andhra Pradesh are major remittance-receiving states; (3) High-skill migrants: IT professionals and doctors in the US and UK send substantial savings. Remittances matter for development because: they are more stable than FDI or portfolio flows (personal relationships create commitment); they are counter-cyclical (increase during home-country recessions as migrants send more during family hardship); they fund immediate household needs (food, healthcare, education, housing); in Kerala, remittances drove the 'Kerala model' of human development; nationally, India's remittances contribute approximately 3% of GDP and help support the balance of payments. The World Bank notes that remittances to low- and middle-income countries ($626 billion in 2022) exceed total global ODA ($185 billion) by more than 3:1.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: China as largest recipient (A) is factually wrong - China is third globally; China actually ranks as a major remittance-sending country due to Chinese diaspora; the BRI is funded through China Development Bank and policy bank loans, not diaspora remittances; conflating China's outward investment with diaspora inflows misrepresents both

United States as recipient (C) confuses the US's role as world's largest remittance-sending country with recipient status - the US receives some remittances from Canadians and Europeans but is overwhelmingly a net sender; skilled migrants in the US send earnings to origin countries, not to the US

Mexico as largest recipient (D) was historically accurate (Mexico led for decades) but India surpassed Mexico around 2008; Mexico is second or third globally depending on the year; the US-Mexico corridor is indeed the world's largest bilateral remittance corridor, but India's total from multiple corridors exceeds Mexico's single-corridor dominance

question_text: The 'flying geese' model of development, proposed by Japanese economist Kaname Akamatsu and later elaborated by Kiyoshi Kojima, describes the sequential pattern of East Asian industrialisation. How does this model explain the regional spread of manufacturing from Japan to South Korea/Taiwan to ASEAN to China, and what are its implications for understanding economic globalisation?

options.A: The flying geese model argues that Japanese military conquest of East Asia created involuntary technology transfer, with industrialisation spreading through colonial extraction rather than voluntary trade
options.B: The model describes how as Japan industrialised and its wages rose, labour-intensive manufacturing activities became uncompetitive and were relocated to lower-wage neighbours (South Korea, Taiwan); as these in turn upgraded, production moved further to ASEAN and then China - creating a regional production hierarchy where each economy upgrades its industrial structure over time through sequential waves of FDI and technology transfer
options.C: The flying geese model predicts that all East Asian economies will converge to identical income levels and industrial structures within 50 years, making trade within the region obsolete
options.D: The model describes a zero-sum competition where each new entrant into manufacturing displaces the previous leader, resulting in permanent deindustrialisation of Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan as China absorbed all regional manufacturing

explanation: Kaname Akamatsu's 'flying geese' (ganko keitai) model (1930s, elaborated post-war) describes industrial upgrading in a V-formation: the lead goose (Japan) flies ahead, followed by South Korea and Taiwan, then ASEAN (Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia), and more recently China and Vietnam. The mechanism: as Japan's wages rose through successful industrialisation, low-technology labour-intensive industries (textiles, basic electronics assembly) became uncompetitive in Japan; Japanese firms relocated production to South Korea and Taiwan through FDI and technology licensing, maintaining higher-value activities at home. South Korea and Taiwan underwent the same process, upgrading from textiles to electronics to semiconductors (TSMC, Samsung) while relocating labour-intensive assembly to Malaysia and Thailand. China's WTO accession (2001) and enormous labour supply made it the dominant recipient of relocated manufacturing through the 2000s. The model's implications for globalisation theory: (1) FDI from advanced economies can enable genuine industrialisation (technology transfer, not just assembly); (2) Regional integration facilitates sequential upgrading; (3) The model is optimistic about catch-up development through manufacturing-export strategy. Critics note that the 'China effect' may have broken the sequential pattern by absorbing so much manufacturing that ASEAN countries found upgrading more difficult; also, automation and digital technologies may end the viability of low-wage manufacturing as a development pathway for later-industrialising countries (the 'premature deindustrialisation' argument by Dani Rodrik).
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Japanese military conquest as transfer mechanism (A) would describe the colonial era; the flying geese model specifically describes post-war voluntary economic integration through trade and FDI; Japan's post-war development was built on peaceful integration into US-led liberal economic order, not colonial extraction

Convergence to identical structures (C) is not what the model predicts - it predicts sequential DIFFERENTIATION as lead economies upgrade while others take over lower-value activities; the model is about dynamic comparative advantage shifts, not convergence to homogeneity

Zero-sum permanent deindustrialisation (D) mischaracterises the model - Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan have all upgraded into higher-value activities (semiconductors, precision engineering, pharmaceuticals, design) rather than experiencing permanent deindustrialisation; Sony, Samsung, and TSMC are global leaders; the model is explicitly about upgrading, not displacement

question_text: The concept of 'financialisation' describes a transformation in advanced capitalist economies since the 1970s where the financial sector's share of GDP, profits, and economic activity has grown dramatically relative to manufacturing and goods production. What are the key indicators and consequences of financialisation that have been identified by economists like Gerald Epstein and Thomas Palley?

options.A: Financialisation refers to the expansion of online banking and mobile payment systems that has reduced the cost of financial transactions for ordinary consumers globally
options.B: Financialisation describes the growing dominance of financial motives, financial markets, financial actors, and financial institutions in domestic and international economic operations - measured by finance's rising GDP share, the shift of non-financial corporations to financial activities, short-termism in corporate governance, and growing household debt; associated with rising inequality and economic instability
options.C: Financialisation is exclusively a positive development because expanding capital markets increases investment efficiency and enables more productive allocation of resources to their highest-value uses
options.D: Financialisation only affects countries with developed capital markets like the US and UK - developing countries with smaller financial sectors are completely immune from its effects

explanation: Gerald Epstein's foundational definition (2005): 'the increasing role of financial motives, financial markets, financial actors, and financial institutions in the operation of domestic and international economies.' Key indicators and mechanisms: (1) Finance GDP share: In the US, financial sector profits rose from approximately 10% of total corporate profits in the 1950s to over 40% by the early 2000s; (2) Non-financial corporations go financial: manufacturing companies like GE Capital (General Electric's financial arm) generated more profits than GE's industrial operations; companies prioritised share buybacks over productive investment; (3) Short-termism: Jack Welch's 'shareholder value maximisation' doctrine pressured managers to produce quarterly earnings at the expense of long-term investment, R&D, and employment stability; (4) Household debt: Rising household debt substituted for wage growth to maintain consumption - US household debt-to-GDP rose from ~50% in 1980 to ~100% by 2007; (5) Inequality: Financial sector compensation grew far faster than other wages; financial asset ownership is concentrated at the top quintile; (6) Instability: Minsky's 'financial instability hypothesis' (leverage cycles leading to speculative and Ponzi finance phases) became increasingly relevant. Thomas Palley, Hyman Minsky, and Andrew Haldane's research documented these dynamics. The 2008 crisis was partly a financialisation crisis: complex financial instruments, excessive leverage, and financial sector opacity created systemic risk that overwhelmed the real economy.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Online banking and mobile payments (A) describes fintech disruption of retail banking - a very different phenomenon from financialisation's structural dominance of the economy; fintech reduces transaction costs but financialisation is about the macroeconomic power shift toward financial sector interests in investment, corporate governance, and household debt

Exclusively positive capital allocation (C) is the efficient markets hypothesis view that financialisation optimises resource allocation - but this ignores documented instability (Minsky cycles), short-termism in investment, and inequality effects; the 2008 crisis was specifically a counterexample of financial efficiency

Only affects US and UK (D) is empirically wrong - through dollar hegemony, global capital flows, and the 'carry trade', financialisation dynamics affect developing countries; India's Sensex movements correlate with US Fed policy; 'sudden stops' of portfolio capital affect all emerging markets; the 2008 crisis spread globally precisely through financial channels

question_text: Special Economic Zones (SEZs) have been used by many countries as instruments for integrating into global trade and investment networks while managing the pace and extent of market opening. China's four original SEZs established in 1980 (Shenzhen, Zhuhai, Shantou, Xiamen) are the most celebrated examples. What made China's SEZ model strategically significant?

options.A: China's SEZs were primarily military bases that allowed foreign armies to train Chinese forces in exchange for technology transfer - their economic function was secondary to defence cooperation
options.B: China's SEZs were enclave economies where foreign investment, flexible labour rules, and export-oriented production could be tested and scaled without immediately extending reform to the entire country - Shenzhen's transformation from a fishing village to a $400 billion economy demonstrated the viability of export-led development and created political momentum for broader opening
options.C: China's SEZs were exclusive areas for domestic Chinese companies only - foreign companies were explicitly banned from SEZs under Deng Xiaoping's ideology of self-reliant development
options.D: China's SEZs were identical to India's Special Economic Zones created under the SEZ Act 2005 - both used the same policy framework with similar outcomes

explanation: China's SEZ model under Deng Xiaoping (initiated 1979, formally established 1980) was strategically brilliant as a reform laboratory. Shenzhen, adjacent to Hong Kong, exemplifies the transformation: from approximately 30,000 people in 1979 to 12+ million people and $480 billion GDP by 2022, it became home to Huawei, Tencent, DJI, and hundreds of major technology companies. The SEZ design solved a key political-economic problem: reforming China's entire planned economy at once would face massive resistance from state enterprise workers, Communist Party apparatchiks, and ideological conservatives. SEZs allowed capitalism to be 'tested' in geographically bounded areas without threatening the existing system. Foreign companies (initially from Hong Kong, Taiwan, then from the US, Japan, Europe) were permitted under rules not applicable elsewhere in China: market-determined wages, flexible hiring and firing, profit repatriation, tax holidays. Technology transfer occurred through joint ventures with Chinese partners, reverse engineering, and training of Chinese workers who then left to start their own companies. Successful results created political demonstration effects - other coastal cities (Shanghai, Guangzhou) and eventually inland areas were opened progressively. India's SEZ Act 2005 was modelled partly on China but produced very different outcomes (D is wrong) - India's SEZs faced land acquisition conflicts, inadequate infrastructure, and debates about domestic tariff area advantages.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Military bases for defence cooperation (A) is completely fictional - China's SEZs were civilian economic zones from their inception; Shenzhen's transformation was explicitly an economic experiment; no military training exchange was part of the SEZ model

Domestic companies only (C) directly contradicts the SEZ definition and purpose - the entire point of SEZs was to attract FOREIGN direct investment; foreign companies were the primary target; without FDI, the technology transfer and export-led growth model would not have worked

Identical to India's SEZ Act 2005 with similar outcomes (D) is empirically wrong - China's SEZs were established 25 years earlier (1980 vs 2005), in a fundamentally different political-economic context, with far stronger state capacity to provide infrastructure and manage labour; India's SEZs generated significant controversy over land acquisition (Nandigram, Singur) and produced much more modest results

question_text: The TRIPS Agreement (Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights) under the WTO has been one of the most contested elements of economic globalisation. India and Brazil's challenge to TRIPS in the context of access to medicines for HIV/AIDS treatment became a landmark in the globalisation-development debate. What was the core issue and how was it resolved?

options.A: India argued that the TRIPS agreement violated national sovereignty because it required India to extend patents to software, which India claimed should be free for educational purposes
options.B: India and Brazil challenged TRIPS's 20-year pharmaceutical patents by arguing that TRIPS violated developing countries' public health rights - their production of generic antiretrovirals at $300/year vs brand-name at $10,000-15,000/year made HIV treatment accessible to millions; the 2001 Doha Declaration affirmed TRIPS flexibilities for public health and the 2003 TRIPS waiver allowed production for export to countries without manufacturing capacity
options.C: India won a complete exemption from TRIPS requirements by demonstrating at the WTO dispute settlement body that Indian traditional medicine predated all pharmaceutical patent claims
options.D: The TRIPS issue was resolved when the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation paid pharmaceutical companies to donate brand-name antiretrovirals to Africa, eliminating the need for generic production

explanation: The HIV/AIDS access-to-medicines crisis of the late 1990s exposed TRIPS's most controversial consequence. TRIPS Article 27 requires WTO members to grant 20-year patent protection for pharmaceutical products. Western pharmaceutical companies (GlaxoSmithKline, Bristol-Myers Squibb, Merck, Roche) held patents on key antiretrovirals (ARVs); the annual cost per patient was $10,000-15,000 - inaccessible for the vast majority of the 25 million people in sub-Saharan Africa needing treatment. India's pharmaceutical industry, particularly companies like Cipla (led by Yusuf Hamied) and Ranbaxy, had developed generic ARV formulations costing approximately $295-350 per patient per year by 2001. Brazil had invoked compulsory licensing to produce generic ARVs domestically under TRIPS Article 31. The South African Treatment Action Campaign and MSF (Medecins Sans Frontieres) built global civil society pressure. The 1998 lawsuit by 39 pharmaceutical companies against South Africa's Medicines Act was withdrawn in 2001 under massive public pressure. At Doha in November 2001, WTO members adopted the 'Doha Declaration on the TRIPS Agreement and Public Health' affirming that TRIPS should not prevent members from taking measures to protect public health, and that countries have the right to grant compulsory licences. The 2003 TRIPS waiver (implemented as permanent amendment in 2017) allowed countries with manufacturing capacity (India, Brazil) to export compulsory-licensed generic medicines to countries without such capacity. Cipla's offers dramatically reduced ARV prices globally.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Software patents and educational exemption (A) is a different TRIPS issue - India has indeed maintained narrow patentability criteria under Section 3(d) of the Patents Act (especially relevant for pharmaceutical evergreening), but the specific HIV/AIDS access crisis was about pharmaceutical product patents, not software; the Doha Declaration specifically addressed public health, not software

Complete exemption through traditional medicine prior art (C) is fictional - no WTO member has ever received a complete TRIPS exemption based on traditional knowledge claims; India has used traditional knowledge documentation (TKDL) to challenge specific patents at patent offices, but this operates case-by-case, not as a blanket exemption

Gates Foundation donation solution (D) describes a real philanthropic initiative (PEPFAR, UNITAID, The Global Fund funded ARV access) but philanthropy did not resolve the TRIPS issue - the WTO-level resolution through the Doha Declaration and compulsory licensing framework was the structural policy change; philanthropy funded procurement, not the legal framework enabling generic production

question_text: India's software and IT services sector - represented by companies like TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and HCL Technologies - has grown from nearly zero in 1990 to over $190 billion in annual exports by 2022. What combination of factors explains why India developed a globally competitive IT services sector rather than the manufacturing-led export model that drove East Asian growth?

options.A: India deliberately chose IT services over manufacturing as a strategic planning decision made by the Planning Commission in 1985, which correctly predicted the internet's importance
options.B: India's IT success reflected specific factor endowments and historical accidents: English language proficiency (colonial legacy enabling communication with US/UK clients), large pool of English-medium engineering graduates from IITs and NITs, time zone advantage for 24/7 service delivery, and Y2K crisis (1990s) that provided initial large-scale outsourcing contracts; combined with infrastructure constraints that made manufacturing less competitive
options.C: India's IT sector was entirely created by US companies who relocated their technology workers to India through mass migration schemes, with no organic domestic entrepreneurship
options.D: India's IT sector success proves that developing countries should not invest in manufacturing and should focus exclusively on services - India's model is universally replicable across all developing economies

explanation: India's IT success reflects a specific combination of supply and demand factors that were partially accidental and partially structural. Supply side: (1) English language: British colonial education created a large English-proficient population - critical for serving US and UK markets; (2) Engineering talent: IITs established from the late 1950s (often drawing on MIT's model) produced world-class engineers; by 2000 India was graduating 400,000+ engineering students annually; (3) Time zone: India's IST is approximately 9-13 hours ahead of US time zones, enabling 'follow-the-sun' operations where Indian teams continue work on projects during US night hours. Demand side: (1) Y2K crisis (1999): fear that legacy computer systems would fail at the year 2000 created enormous demand for programmers to audit and fix old COBOL code; Indian IT companies like TCS and Infosys won large Y2K contracts that established their credibility; (2) Dot-com boom demand for software development; (3) Cost arbitrage: Indian engineers could be hired at 15-20% of US salary while offering comparable skills. Infrastructure constraints: India's inadequate road networks, ports, power reliability, and land acquisition difficulties made manufacturing FDI less attractive compared to China's infrastructure advantage. Dani Rodrik has argued that India's services-led model is atypical and not easily replicable for countries without similar factor endowments - 'services cannot be the escalator for development that manufacturing has been.'
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: 1985 Planning Commission strategic choice (A) is historically wrong - India's IT sector was not the product of central planning; the Planning Commission did not predict the internet; the sector emerged through private entrepreneurship (Narayana Murthy founding Infosys 1981, Wipro's pivot to software); government policy was more enabling than directive

US companies relocating workers to India (C) misrepresents the sector's character - the IT sector was built by Indian entrepreneurs and companies; TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and HCL are Indian-owned companies that built global delivery models; the flow was primarily of WORK going to India, not US workers moving to India

Universal replicability of India's model (D) is contested by development economists - Rodrik specifically argues that services-led development is harder to generalise because it requires specific factor endowments (English proficiency, engineering education systems) that took decades to build; Bangladesh, Ethiopia, and most developing countries cannot replicate India's IT path without those specific preconditions

question_text: The G20, established as a forum for Finance Ministers in 1999 and elevated to Leaders' Summit level in 2008, has become the primary forum for global economic governance. Why did the G7 prove insufficient as the primary global economic governance forum and what does the G20's composition tell us about the changing distribution of global economic power?

options.A: The G7 was sufficient for global economic governance until 2008; it was replaced only because the G7 countries collectively decided to include emerging markets for diplomatic rather than economic reasons
options.B: The G7 (US, UK, France, Germany, Japan, Italy, Canada) represents approximately 40% of global GDP - insufficient for coordinating responses to crises that increasingly originate in or require engagement from emerging market economies (China, India, Brazil, South Africa, Indonesia) that collectively account for growing shares of global output, trade, and financial flows
options.C: The G20 replaced the G7 because the United Nations General Assembly passed a binding resolution requiring the creation of a new economic governance forum with universal membership
options.D: The G7 was replaced because European countries collectively left the G7 to form a separate EU economic bloc, reducing the G7 to only three countries

explanation: The G20 (comprising 19 countries plus the EU) was established as a Finance Ministers forum in 1999 in direct response to the 1997-98 Asian Financial Crisis, which demonstrated that emerging market economies had become systemically important enough that global financial stability could not be managed without them. The elevation to Leaders' Summit in November 2008 (Washington Summit) was specifically triggered by the Global Financial Crisis - it was clear that the G7's coordinated bank bailout packages and fiscal stimulus needed to include China, India, Brazil, South Africa, and Indonesia to be globally effective. The composition shift reflects real economic weight changes: China had grown from approximately 4% of global GDP (PPP) in 1990 to approximately 18% by 2008; India from 3% to 6%; Brazil, South Korea, Indonesia, and Saudi Arabia all became top-20 economies. The G20 collectively represents approximately 85% of global GDP, 75% of global trade, and two-thirds of the world's population - making it far more representative of actual economic power than the G7 (approximately 40% of global GDP by market rates). India's G20 presidency in 2023 elevated the African Union to permanent G20 membership - a significant expansion reflecting Africa's growing demographic and economic weight. The G20 operates by consensus and lacks enforcement authority, making it primarily a coordination forum rather than a governing institution.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: G7 sufficient until 2008 and replaced diplomatically (A) ignores the 1999 Finance Ministers G20 creation - the insufficiency of the G7 was recognised after the 1997-98 Asian Financial Crisis, nine years before the 2008 crisis elevated it to Leaders' level; calling the expansion purely diplomatic ignores the growing economic weight of emerging markets documented in GDP data

UNGA binding resolution (C) is fictional - the G20 is an informal forum with no treaty basis; the UNGA cannot create binding governance institutions outside the UN system; the G20 was established through consensus among invited finance ministers, not through any formal UN mechanism

European countries leaving G7 (D) is factually wrong - France, Germany, Italy, and the UK remain full G7 members; no European country has left the G7; EU is separately represented in G20 discussions but G7 European members remain as individual countries

question_text:
Match the following economic globalisation concepts in Column A with their correct descriptions in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) | (P) Long-term cross-border capital investment (at least 10% ownership) creating productive assets; includes greenfield investment and mergers/acquisitions; transfers technology, management, supply chain access |
| (ii) Special Economic Zones (SEZs) | (Q) Geographically bounded enclaves with special regulations for foreign investment and export production - reduced tariffs, flexible labour rules, tax holidays; China's Shenzhen is the iconic example |
| (iii) Transfer Pricing | (R) Intra-firm pricing between subsidiaries of the same multinational; when manipulated to shift profits from high-tax to low-tax jurisdictions, reduces host country tax revenues; addressed by OECD BEPS project |
| (iv) TRIPS Agreement | (S) WTO agreement requiring 20-year patents for pharmaceutical products; contested in HIV/AIDS access crisis; 2001 Doha Declaration affirmed public health flexibilities; India's generics industry central to debate |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Long-term cross-border capital investment (at least 10% ownership) creating productive assets; includes greenfield investment and mergers/acquisitions; transfers technology, management, supply chain access
Geographically bounded enclaves with special regulations for foreign investment and export production - reduced tariffs, flexible labour rules, tax holidays; China's Shenzhen is the iconic example

Intra-firm pricing between subsidiaries of the same multinational; when manipulated to shift profits from high-tax to low-tax jurisdictions, reduces host country tax revenues; addressed by OECD BEPS project

WTO agreement requiring 20-year patents for pharmaceutical products; contested in HIV/AIDS access crisis; 2001 Doha Declaration affirmed public health flexibilities; India's generics industry central to debate

explanation: FDI (i) - defined by IMF/OECD as minimum 10% ownership stake; distinguished from portfolio investment by control dimension and long-term commitment; includes greenfield (building new facilities), brownfield (acquiring existing), and M&A; source of technology transfer, employment, and supply chain integration (P). SEZs (ii) - geographically bounded special regulatory areas; China's Shenzhen (1980), India's export processing zones, Bangladesh's garment EPZs, and Vietnam's industrial parks are examples; key features are export orientation, special tax treatment, and regulatory flexibility (Q). Transfer pricing (iii) - intra-group transaction pricing; arm's length principle is the international standard; OECD BEPS Action Plan (15 actions, 2015) addressed base erosion through transfer pricing; India's TPO (Transfer Pricing Officer) system handles domestic enforcement (R). TRIPS (iv) - Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights, 1994; Article 27 pharmaceutical patents; Article 31 compulsory licensing; contested by India and Brazil in HIV/AIDS context; Cipla's $295/patient generics challenged brand-name $10,000+ prices (S).
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: FDI and transfer pricing are both about MNC finance but different aspects - FDI is the initial investment flow creating ownership and productive assets; transfer pricing is the internal accounting manipulation between already-established subsidiaries; timing and mechanism differ completely

SEZs and TRIPS are both globalisation governance tools but in different domains - SEZs are geographic industrial policy tools for FDI attraction; TRIPS is an intellectual property rights framework within WTO; Shenzhen uniquely identifies SEZs while HIV/AIDS generics uniquely identifies TRIPS

BEPS (R) is the unique identifier for transfer pricing - OECD's Base Erosion and Profit Shifting Action Plan was specifically created in response to transfer pricing abuses; any option mentioning BEPS must map to transfer pricing, not to TRIPS or SEZs

question_text:
Match the following economic globalisation events/crises in Column A with their correct causes and lessons in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) East Asian Financial Crisis (1997-98) | (P) US subprime mortgage defaults securitised into CDOs distributed globally; Lehman Brothers collapse froze credit markets; deepest global recession since 1930s; demonstrated systemic risk of financial globalisation |
| (ii) Global Financial Crisis (2008) | (Q) Rapid currency and capital flow reversals in Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea; triggered by premature capital account liberalisation without domestic financial sector development; IMF's contractionary conditionality worsened recessions |
| (iii) Argentina's Default (2001-02) | (R) Currency board (peso-dollar peg) collapsed after capital flight; $100 billion sovereign default; IMF standby programs proved inadequate; cacerolazo protests; demonstrated limits of fixed exchange rates under capital account openness |
| (iv) Greek Debt Crisis (2010-15) | (S) Eurozone member unable to devalue currency or control monetary policy; required three bailout packages from IMF/ECB/ESM; severe austerity produced 25% GDP contraction; demonstrated contradictions of monetary union without fiscal union |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: US subprime mortgage defaults securitised into CDOs distributed globally; Lehman Brothers collapse froze credit markets; deepest global recession since 1930s; demonstrated systemic risk of financial globalisation

Rapid currency and capital flow reversals in Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea; triggered by premature capital account liberalisation without domestic financial sector development; IMF contractionary conditionality worsened recessions

Currency board (peso-dollar peg) collapsed after capital flight; $100 billion sovereign default; IMF standby programs proved inadequate; cacerolazo protests; demonstrated limits of fixed exchange rates under capital account openness

Eurozone member unable to devalue currency or control monetary policy; required three bailout packages from IMF/ECB/ESM; severe austerity produced 25% GDP contraction; demonstrated contradictions of monetary union without fiscal union

explanation: East Asian Crisis (i) - started with Thai baht devaluation July 1997; spread through Indonesia (Suharto resignation), South Korea (IMF $57B bailout), Malaysia (Mahathir's capital controls); the crisis was triggered by premature capital account liberalisation; IMF conditionality (raise interest rates, cut fiscal spending during a recession) was strongly criticised by Stiglitz and Jeffrey Sachs; Mahathir's Malaysian capital controls were vindicated by faster recovery (Q). Global Financial Crisis 2008 (ii) - US housing market collapse, CDO/MBS securitisation, global distribution of toxic assets, Lehman Brothers September 15, 2008; global credit freeze; emerging market contagion; governments responded with unprecedented bailouts ($700B TARP, QE) (P). Argentina 2001-02 (iii) - Argentina maintained a currency board pegging the peso 1:1 to the dollar from 1991; by 2001 the overvalued peso made exports uncompetitive; capital flight led to bank runs; Argentina defaulted on $100 billion of public debt (largest sovereign default in history at the time); the default was followed by 4 devaluations in 2 weeks; 'cacerolazo' protests forced five presidents in two weeks (R). Greek crisis (iv) - Greece's euro membership meant it could not devalue or print money during the 2008 crisis; underlying fiscal unsustainability (hidden deficits revealed in 2009) triggered bond market crisis; three bailout programs (2010, 2012, 2015); unemployment reached 27%; debate about Eurozone design (S).
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Asian Crisis and GFC 2008 are both financial crises but different mechanisms - Asian Crisis = capital flow reversal in emerging markets triggered by premature liberalisation; GFC 2008 = US mortgage securitisation creating global systemic risk; Lehman Brothers uniquely identifies 2008; Thailand/baht uniquely identifies Asian Crisis

Argentina and Greece are both debt/currency crises but different contexts - Argentina had a dollar-peg currency board that collapsed; Greece was in the Eurozone and couldn't devalue; 'cacerolazo' (pot-banging protests) is the unique Argentine marker; 'monetary union without fiscal union' is the Greek/Eurozone marker

Asian Crisis and Argentina both involved IMF programs - but Asian Crisis involved capital flow reversal in 4 countries simultaneously; Argentina was a single country sovereign default; the magnitude ($100 billion sovereign default) and currency board mechanism uniquely identify Argentina

question_text:
Match the following key economists and their primary contributions to understanding economic globalisation in Column A with their correct frameworks in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) Paul Krugman | (P) 'The Globalisation Paradox' (2011) - countries cannot simultaneously maximise economic integration, sovereignty, and democratic governance; 'smart globalisation' accepts shallower integration to preserve democratic legitimacy |
| (ii) Dani Rodrik | (Q) New Trade Theory (1979) - economies of scale and imperfect competition mean trade patterns are determined by historical accident and increasing returns, not just comparative advantage; 1991 geography of economic activity |
| (iii) Joseph Stiglitz | (R) 'Globalisation and its Discontents' (2002) - IMF ideology overrode evidence in Asian crisis management; calls for reformed globalisation with human face; market failures require government intervention |
| (iv) Ha-Joon Chang | (S) 'Kicking Away the Ladder' (2002) - today's rich countries developed through protectionism and industrial policy, then kicked away the ladder by demanding free trade from developing countries through WTO and conditionality |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: 'The Globalisation Paradox' (2011) - countries cannot simultaneously maximise economic integration, sovereignty, and democratic governance; smart globalisation accepts shallower integration to preserve democratic legitimacy

New Trade Theory (1979) - economies of scale and imperfect competition mean trade patterns are determined by historical accident and increasing returns, not just comparative advantage; 1991 geography of economic activity

'Globalisation and its Discontents' (2002) - IMF ideology overrode evidence in Asian crisis management; calls for reformed globalisation with human face; market failures require government intervention

'Kicking Away the Ladder' (2002) - today's rich countries developed through protectionism and industrial policy, then kicked away the ladder by demanding free trade from developing countries through WTO and conditionality

explanation: Paul Krugman (i) - Nobel Prize 2008; New Trade Theory (1979 paper 'Increasing Returns, Monopolistic Competition and International Trade') challenged Heckscher-Ohlin factor endowment models by showing that economies of scale create first-mover advantages explaining why Germany and Japan both export cars; 1991 book 'Geography and Trade' applied similar logic to spatial concentration of industries (Q). Dani Rodrik (ii) - Harvard economist; 'The Globalisation Paradox' articulated the trilemma of deep integration vs sovereignty vs democracy; earlier 'Has Globalisation Gone Too Far?' (1997) presciently identified distributional and stability concerns; 'Straight Talk on Trade' (2017) updated the argument post-Trump (P). Joseph Stiglitz (iii) - Nobel 2001, World Bank Chief Economist 1997-2000 (resigned in protest over IMF's Asia approach); 'Globalisation and its Discontents' (2002) is the foundational insider critique; 'Making Globalisation Work' (2006) presents reform agenda (R). Ha-Joon Chang (iv) - Cambridge economist from South Korea; 'Kicking Away the Ladder' (2002) showed through historical analysis that Britain industrialised through Navigation Acts and protectionism; the US used tariffs throughout the 19th century; Germany's Zollverein protected infant industries - all contrary to free trade principles they now preach; '23 Things They Don't Tell You About Capitalism' (2010) popular extension (S).
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Krugman and Rodrik are both Harvard-associated economists critical of simple globalisation narratives - Krugman's contribution is New Trade Theory (economies of scale, 1979, Nobel 2008); Rodrik's is the globalisation trilemma and democratic legitimacy; book titles are distinguishing markers: 'The Globalisation Paradox' = Rodrik, not Krugman

Stiglitz and Chang both critique IMF/WTO orthodoxy from a development perspective - Stiglitz as insider (former World Bank Chief Economist critiquing IMF); Chang as historian showing rich-country hypocrisy; 'Discontents' = Stiglitz; 'Kicking Away the Ladder' = Chang

Ha-Joon Chang's unique contribution is the historical argument about protectionism in today's developed countries - his work specifically shows through case studies of Britain, US, Germany, Japan that they industrialised through the same protectionist policies they now condemn; this historical-hypocrisy argument is distinctive from Stiglitz's IMF-crisis critique

question_text:
Examine the following Assertion and Reason carefully:

Assertion (A): The 'China shock' - the rapid increase in Chinese imports to advanced economies following China's WTO accession in 2001 - had more severe and persistent negative effects on US manufacturing employment than standard trade theory predicted, and directly contributed to the political conditions enabling populist anti-trade politics.

Reason (R): Economists David Autor, David Dorn, and Gordon Hanson (2013, 2016) documented that US manufacturing regions exposed to Chinese import competition lost employment at rates far higher than adjustments occurred, with workers unable to transition to new employment due to geographic immobility, skills mismatch, and the destruction of entire local economic ecosystems - violating the standard trade model assumption that displaced workers find equivalent employment in expanding export sectors.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

explanation: Both A and R are true and causally linked. The Assertion (A) makes two claims: (1) the China shock produced larger and more persistent job losses than standard trade theory predicted; (2) this contributed to anti-trade populism. Both are well-supported. Standard Heckscher-Ohlin trade theory predicts factor price equalisation and Heckscher-Ohlin mobility assumptions suggest displaced workers will find equivalent employment in export-expanding sectors. The Reason (R) accurately describes the Autor-Dorn-Hanson ('China Syndrome') research programme. Their 2013 paper in the American Economic Review, followed by 2016 work on electoral consequences, established: (1) Employment losses in import-competing manufacturing (furniture in North Carolina, textiles in the Carolinas, electronics in the Midwest) were 2-3x larger than models predicted; (2) Geographic immobility - workers in declining manufacturing regions did not move to growing service sectors as models assumed; (3) Skills specificity - manufacturing workers had industry-specific skills not easily transferable; (4) Local multiplier collapse - manufacturing job losses reduced service sector employment in the same communities through reduced local demand; (5) Political consequences: Autor et al. (2020) found that counties more exposed to Chinese import competition showed greater support for Republican (Trump) and Democratic (Sanders) candidates with populist, anti-trade positions. R directly explains the empirical mechanism underlying A's claim, making this A+R true with R explaining A.
subject: political-science

question_text:
Examine the following Assertion and Reason carefully:

Assertion (A): The G20's elevation to Leaders' Summit level in November 2008 represented a genuine shift in global economic governance from G7 domination toward more inclusive multipolarity - reflecting the changed distribution of global economic power.

Reason (R): The 2008 Washington G20 Summit was called specifically because G7 leaders recognised that addressing the global financial crisis required the participation of China, India, Brazil, and other major emerging economies that collectively controlled critical shares of global foreign exchange reserves, trade flows, and economic growth capacity.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

explanation: Both A and R are accurate and causally connected. The Assertion (A) correctly identifies G20 elevation as reflecting genuine multipolarity - the shift from G7 to G20 was driven by economic reality (emerging markets' growing share of global GDP, trade, and reserves) rather than purely symbolic inclusion. The G20 collectively represents approximately 85% of global GDP vs G7's ~40% - making it genuinely more representative of global economic power distribution. The Reason (R) accurately explains why the Washington Summit was convened in that form: Chinese foreign exchange reserves had reached $2 trillion by 2008, providing critical stabilisation capacity; India, Brazil, and China maintained positive growth through 2009 when G7 economies contracted, demonstrating their weight; the coordinated $5 trillion fiscal stimulus that the G20 agreed required emerging market participation to be globally effective. R precisely explains A's claim about genuine (not symbolic) governance shift - the inclusion was necessitated by practical crisis management needs reflecting changed economic power distribution. The causal chain is tight: changed economic power distribution (R's precondition) > crisis requiring broader coordination (R's mechanism) > G20 elevation as governance response (A's observation).
subject: political-science

question_text:
Examine the following Assertion and Reason carefully:

Assertion (A): India's position in international trade negotiations has been shaped by a fundamental tension between its interest as a large domestic market seeking market access for its services exports and its need to protect agricultural livelihoods for 600+ million rural population from import competition.

Reason (R): India is simultaneously a major services exporter (IT/BPO, software, financial services) seeking liberalisation in GATS Mode 1 (cross-border supply) and Mode 4 (movement of natural persons) while also being one of the most vocal defenders of the right to food security through public stockholding programs that are classified as trade-distorting 'amber box' support under WTO agricultural rules.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

explanation: Both A and R are accurate and R precisely explains the dual interest dynamic that creates the tension A describes. The Assertion (A) correctly identifies India's dual positioning: as both a market-access seeker (services) and a defensive player (agriculture). The Reason (R) provides specific WTO legal content explaining both sides: On services: India is the world's leading IT/BPO services exporter and has pushed hard in Doha negotiations for Mode 1 (cross-border online services, where India has comparative advantage) and Mode 4 (temporary movement of service professionals - H1-B visas, intra-company transfers) liberalisation; developed countries (particularly the US, EU) have resisted Mode 4 liberalisation citing immigration concerns. On agriculture: India procures foodgrains (rice, wheat) through Food Corporation of India at Minimum Support Prices (MSP) for the National Food Security Act - classifying this as 'amber box' (production-distorting) support; India's procurement values have exceeded the WTO's 10% of production value de minimis ceiling, creating a legal exposure that India challenged at Bali (2013) securing a 'Peace Clause' and later at Nairobi (2015) securing a permanent solution mandate. This dual demandeur-defensive posture directly explains why A's tension exists, with R providing the specific WTO mechanism details.
subject: political-science

question_text:
The following events trace the evolution of India's trade and investment policies from protectionism to globalisation. Arrange them in the correct chronological sequence:

(i) India joins WTO as founding member, accepting TRIPS, GATS, and dispute settlement obligations
(ii) Industrial Policy Resolution establishes the 'License Raj' framework - state permission required for major investment decisions; import substitution model takes hold
(iii) India launches Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for 14 sectors to attract manufacturing FDI and boost exports
(iv) New Economic Policy announced - industrial licensing abolished for most sectors, FDI rules liberalised, import duties reduced dramatically
(v) India's IT/BPO exports cross $100 billion - India becomes world's largest services exporter by employee headcount
items: (i) India joins WTO as founding member
(ii) Industrial Policy Resolution - License Raj established
(iv) New Economic Policy - licensing abolished, FDI liberalised
explanation: India's trade policy chronology: (ii) 1956 - The Industrial Policy Resolution 1956 (building on 1948 version) established the License Raj framework under PM Jawaharlal Nehru; industries were divided into three schedules (state monopoly, state priority, private permitted with licenses); every significant investment, expansion, and technology import required government approval; import substitution industrialisation was the explicit strategy. (iv) July 1991 - New Economic Policy launched by Finance Minister Manmohan Singh under PM Narasimha Rao; industrial licensing abolished for most sectors (only 18 industries remained licensed, down from 63); automatic approval for FDI up to 51% in most sectors; import duties reduced from average 87% to 47%; MRTP Act restrictions relaxed; the foundational liberalisation moment. (i) January 1, 1995 - India became a founding WTO member along with 122 other countries; India had been a GATT contracting party since 1947 but WTO membership brought new obligations including TRIPS (20-year pharmaceutical patents), GATS (services liberalisation schedule), and the binding DSU. (v) By 2021-22 - India's software and IT/BPO exports crossed $150 billion (NASSCOM estimate) with TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL Technologies being global leaders; this represents the cumulative development of two decades of reforms enabling the sector. (iii) 2020-21 - PLI schemes announced across 14 sectors (mobile phones, pharmaceuticals, automobiles, solar PV, textiles, food processing, etc.); designed to attract $520 billion of production over 5 years; represents the current phase of manufacturing-led globalisation strategy.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: License Raj (1956) always comes first - India's protectionist phase was established before liberalisation; the 1991 NEP explicitly dismantled what the 1956 Resolution built; placing any liberalisation event before 1956 would reverse India's economic history

WTO founding membership (1995) comes after the 1991 NEP but before IT export maturity - India implemented reforms first, then formalised international commitments; the 1991 reforms enabled the services sector growth that produced the $100B+ IT exports in the 2010s

PLI scheme (2020-21) is definitively last as the most recent event - it represents India's current manufacturing strategy; placing PLI before WTO membership (1995) or IT export maturity (2010s) would be chronologically impossible

question_text:
The following events describe the financial crisis contagion and policy response during the 2008 Global Financial Crisis. Arrange them in the correct chronological sequence:

(i) Lehman Brothers files for Chapter 11 bankruptcy - global credit markets freeze
(ii) G20 Leaders' Summit in Washington DC agrees coordinated fiscal stimulus of $5 trillion
(iii) US Federal Reserve cuts interest rates to near zero and announces first Quantitative Easing (QE) programme
(iv) Bear Stearns collapses - Federal Reserve arranges emergency sale to JPMorgan Chase
(v) US House passes $700 billion Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) after initial rejection
items: (i) Lehman Brothers Chapter 11 - credit markets freeze
(ii) G20 Washington Summit - $5 trillion coordinated stimulus
(iii) Fed cuts rates to zero and announces QE
(iv) Bear Stearns collapses - emergency sale to JPMorgan
(v) TARP $700 billion passed after initial rejection
explanation: The 2008 financial crisis chronology: (iv) March 14-17, 2008 - Bear Stearns, the fifth-largest US investment bank, collapsed due to losses on mortgage-backed securities; in an extraordinary intervention the Federal Reserve provided $29 billion in emergency financing to facilitate Bear's sale to JPMorgan Chase at $10/share (a company that had traded at $170/share); this was the first major Fed intervention demonstrating that 'too big to fail' had become policy. (i) September 15, 2008 - Lehman Brothers filed Chapter 11; unlike Bear Stearns, the Fed/Treasury did not arrange a rescue; the failure of a $600 billion balance sheet firm with counterparty relationships across 80+ countries froze global interbank lending overnight; the TED spread (bank credit risk measure) spiked to 4.6%; global credit markets seized. (v) September-October 2008 - Treasury Secretary Hank Paulson and Fed Chair Ben Bernanke asked Congress for $700 billion to purchase toxic assets from banks; the House initially voted NO on September 29 (the Dow fell 778 points, its largest single-day point drop); a revised bill passed October 3, 2008 after the Senate added sweeteners. (iii) October-December 2008 - The Fed cut the federal funds rate to 0-0.25% (near zero) in December 2008 and announced the first QE programme (purchasing $600 billion in mortgage-backed securities) to provide additional monetary stimulus beyond the zero lower bound. (ii) November 14-15, 2008 - The inaugural G20 Leaders' Summit in Washington (hosted by President Bush) agreed that G20 countries would collectively provide $5 trillion in fiscal stimulus to prevent the recession becoming a depression; the commitment to refrain from trade barriers and to reform financial regulation was also agreed.
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Bear Stearns (March 2008) comes before Lehman (September 2008) - Bear Stearns was the first major casualty indicating the crisis's severity; the Fed's decision to rescue Bear but not Lehman (6 months later) was the defining difference that escalated the crisis; reversing this sequence loses the escalation logic

TARP vote (late September/early October) comes immediately after Lehman's collapse (September 15) - Paulson requested TARP funding as a direct response to the post-Lehman credit freeze; the initial rejection on September 29 and subsequent passage on October 3 occurred within three weeks of Lehman

G20 Summit (November 2008) comes last in this sequence - it was the international coordination response to the US-origin crisis; the domestic US responses (Bear Stearns, Lehman, TARP, Fed QE) all preceded the G20 multilateral response; placing G20 before domestic US interventions would imply international governance preceded the crisis itself

question_text:
Evaluate the following four statements about the economic dimensions of globalisation, and indicate which combination is CORRECT:

(i) India is the world's largest recipient of remittances, receiving approximately $111 billion in 2022, with major inflows from the Gulf states (UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait) and the USA, UK, and Canada.

(ii) The TRIPS Agreement under the WTO requires 20-year patent protection for pharmaceutical products; India's production of generic antiretroviral medicines at $295 per patient per year challenged brand-name prices of $10,000-15,000 - leading to the 2001 Doha Declaration affirming public health flexibilities.

(iii) China's Shenzhen SEZ, established in 1980 as one of four original Special Economic Zones, transformed from a small fishing village into a major city with a GDP exceeding $400 billion within four decades - making it the most successful SEZ example globally.

(iv) The G7 (United States, United Kingdom, France, Germany, Japan, Italy, Canada) was expanded to the G20 in 2008 specifically because G7 countries voluntarily chose to include developing countries for symbolic representation rather than any economic necessity.

options.D: (ii) and (iii) are correct; (i) and (iv) contain factual errors

explanation: Statement (i) TRUE: India has been the world's largest remittance recipient since approximately 2008; $111 billion in 2022 per World Bank data; Gulf states (UAE alone sending approximately $18-20 billion), USA, UK, Canada, and Australia are the major sources; Kerala, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Andhra Pradesh are leading recipient states. Statement (ii) TRUE: TRIPS Article 27 mandates 20-year pharmaceutical patents; Cipla's Yusuf Hamied offered generic ARV triple therapy at $350/patient/year (later $295) vs brand-name $10,000-15,000; the Doha Declaration November 2001 affirmed TRIPS flexibilities for public health; compulsory licensing (Article 31) and parallel importing allowed countries to access affordable medicines. Statement (iii) TRUE: Shenzhen was designated one of four original SEZs by Deng Xiaoping in 1980; it was a border town of approximately 30,000 people adjacent to Hong Kong; it grew to 12+ million residents and GDP exceeding $480 billion by 2022; home to Huawei, Tencent, DJI, and Foxconn's major assembly facilities. Statement (iv) FALSE: The G20's elevation to Leaders' Summit in November 2008 was driven by economic necessity - G7 leaders recognised that the financial crisis required participation of China (which held $2 trillion in foreign reserves), India, Brazil, South Korea, and other emerging economies to coordinate the $5 trillion stimulus; this was practical crisis management, not symbolic inclusion.
subject: political-science

question_text: Study the following table comparing major developing economies on key trade and investment indicators, then answer: Which pattern most strongly supports the argument that there is no single 'globalisation model' - that different economies have achieved development through different modes of integration with distinctive roles in global value chains?

options.A: The data shows all developing economies followed the same export-led manufacturing model, with only minor differences in timing - confirming that there is one universally optimal globalisation path
options.B: The table reveals fundamentally different integration models: China as world factory (manufacturing exports), India as world office (services exports), Vietnam as assembly hub (electronics GVC participation), Bangladesh as single-industry specialist (garments), Saudi Arabia as resource exporter - each achieving different development outcomes through different positions in global value chains with no single template
options.C: The data demonstrates that only countries with natural resource wealth achieved development through globalisation - service and manufacturing exporters show no improvement in living standards
options.D: All developing countries that opened their economies achieved identical GDP growth rates, confirming that globalisation benefits are uniformly distributed and free trade is equally beneficial for all integration strategies

explanation: The comparative data across developing economies reveals fundamentally different integration pathways rather than convergence on a single template. China's integration model: manufacturing-led, FDI-enabled, export-oriented, state-directed; became the world's largest goods exporter and manufacturer across virtually all categories; GDP per capita rose from ~$300 to $12,000. India's model: services-led, IT/BPO domination, high-skill workforce, English language advantage; $190B+ services exports but weaker manufacturing; different social outcomes (stronger for educated urban professionals, weaker for rural and informal workers). Vietnam's model: assembly-stage GVC participation starting with garments (1990s), then electronics (Samsung moved major production from South Korea); $360B+ exports in 2022 from $5B in 1995; achieved middle-income status. Bangladesh's model: extreme specialisation in ready-made garments (85% of exports); Rana Plaza (2013) exposed worker safety issues; remarkable poverty reduction and women's workforce participation despite single-sector concentration. Saudi Arabia's model: resource-led (petroleum), Vision 2030 attempting diversification; high per capita income but structural dependency. Option A's single-model claim is contradicted by the fundamentally different structures visible in the data. Option C's resource-only view is contradicted by India's and Vietnam's services/manufacturing achievements. Option D's identical growth rates contradicts observable divergence between e.g. Bangladesh (~$2,500 GDPpc) and Saudi Arabia (~$30,000).
subject: political-science
elimination_hints: Single universal export-manufacturing model (A) is directly contradicted by India's services-led path - India achieved major development gains through IT/BPO exports, not through manufacturing export dominance; Bangladesh's garments vs China's diversified manufacturing vs Saudi Arabia's oil all represent structurally different integration modes

Resource wealth only produces development (C) is refuted by Bangladesh - Bangladesh has no significant natural resources yet achieved middle-income trajectory through garments; India's IT sector involves no resource extraction; Vietnam's electronics assembly requires no resources; resource-only development is a narrow subset of the observed cases

Identical growth rates for all open economies (D) is empirically false - GDP per capita ranges from ~$2,500 (Bangladesh) to ~$12,000 (China) to ~$30,000 (Saudi Arabia) among the countries in the table; this 12-fold variation demonstrates that openness alone does not produce uniform outcomes - the mode, timing, sector, and institutional context all matter
